text: I'm Dr. Karen Zaghian. I'm a colorectal surgeon in Los Angeles and part of the teaching faculty at Cedars-Sinai. Today's case is a laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy for diverticulitis. Our patient's a sixty-five year old woman with recurrent uncomplicated diverticulitis over the past several years, which has progressively worsened over the past year where she's had five discrete attacks in a period of nine months. Therefore, she's elected to proceed with surgery. Now, while my routine sigmoid colectomy performed laparoscopically involves a laparoscopic ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery, a laparoscopic mobilization of the colon, as well as the splenic flexure, and a laparoscopic distal transection of the rectum, in diverticulitis, this may be tricky due to the significant inflammatory changes we see that are associated with the disease.

Sometimes in diverticulitis surgery, the distal transection has to be performed open so that we can properly assess the distal transection as well as the health of the distal rectum that we are transecting. Another part of this case will be the use of fluorescence angiography to evaluate the proximal colon and its vascular blood supply before we perform the anastomosis. I also perform a routine flexible sigmoidoscopy at the end of the procedure in order to air leak test our anastomosis as well as intraluminally check the anastomosis for defects, blood supply, and bleeding.

The surgeon uses a varus needle to enter the abdomen and checks for any injury. A ten millimeter trocar is placed in the umbilicus, followed by a twelve millimeter trocar in the right lower quadrant, positioned two finger breaths from the ASIS and two up. A five millimeter trocar is placed one hand breath up, ensuring it does not interfere with other trocars. The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg with the left side up. Bowel loops are swept out of the pelvis using bowel graspers to minimize trauma.

The surgeon identifies inflammation and adhesions in the sigmoid colon, suggesting a past abscess. Adhesions are carefully dissected to avoid injury to bowel loops. The inflamed sigmoid segment is targeted for removal, with the surgeon proceeding in a medial to lateral fashion. The sacral promontory and inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) are located, distinguishing the IMA pedicle from the iliac branches.

The surgeon holds the tissue up and uses shears to score the area distal to the iliac, identifying the retroperitoneal plane. The ureter is located to safely ligate the inferior mesenteric artery without injuring retroperitoneal structures. A ten millimeter ligature is used for vessel ligation and blunt dissection, revealing the inflamed retroperitoneal plane due to diverticulitis. The ureter is visible, allowing the surgeon to sweep down the fat plane above.

The surgeon identifies the ureter and sweeps it down along with the retroperitoneum to develop a medial to lateral dissection plane up to the splenic flexure. The peritoneum is opened using a ligature to create more space for dissection, while being cautious of the hypogastric nerves near the IMA takeoff. The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is ligated without skeletonizing the vessel, and care is taken to avoid injuring the ligament of Treitz and the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) during the dissection.

The surgeon identifies the ureter and continues the dissection superiorly, aiming to simplify the lateral dissection across the white line of Toldt. The psoas muscle is visible, indicating the dissection plane is too deep, prompting adjustment to a more superficial retroperitoneal plane. The mesentery is adhesed to the retroperitoneum due to inflammation, resulting in a bloodier field. The surgeon sweeps from top down to avoid injury, considering switching to lateral dissection if the medial approach becomes too challenging.

The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure due to a long segment of diverticulitis involving a fifteen-centimeter section of the colon. The dissection plane is shifted laterally after encountering sticky medial dissection. An air bubble from the medial dissection is visible, aiding the lateral approach. The ureter is identified beneath inflamed tissue, and tension is applied to visualize the dissection area, which is adhered to the sidewall.

The surgeon carefully dissects the adhesed tissue from the sidewall, ensuring not to injure the ureter or colon. A trocar is inserted to aid in retraction and suction during the dissection. The surgeon identifies the ureter's position relative to the colon and sidewall, working medially to avoid damage.

The surgeon carefully performs blunt dissection to avoid cutting structures that could potentially be the ureter, which is adhered to surrounding tissue. The decision is made to mobilize the splenic flexure to gain the necessary length for anastomosis. The surgeon identifies and dissects the band attaching the splenic flexure, ensuring not to extend dissection towards the spleen to prevent injury. The colon is pulled over to facilitate this process.

The surgeon is working in the lesser sac to complete the mobilization of the splenic flexure. The stomach is visible above, and care is taken to avoid injury. The colon is positioned below, and the surgeon identifies and manipulates the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) near the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The ligament of Treitz is noted just medial to the IMV. The surgeon continues to dissect and remove any remaining attachments to fully mobilize the area.

The surgeon assesses the inflamed sigmoid colon and rectum, noting concerns about inflammation extending past the peritoneal reflection. The sigmoid colon appears folded and attached to the perineal reflection or vagina. The surgeon decides to perform a laparoscopic tap block using a StimuQuick needle, injecting a weight-based dose of bupivacaine to prepare for a fan and steel incision.

The surgeon makes a fan and steel incision, cutting through subcutaneous tissues to the anterior sheath and exposing the rectus muscle dorsally. Subfascial flaps are mobilized to avoid cutting the rectus muscle, using a muscle-sparing technique. The pyramidalis muscle is identified and preserved. The rectus muscle is split, and the peritoneum is grabbed. The surgeon uses a bovie to cut through the peritoneum, ensuring not to injure the bladder, and employs an Alexis O wound protector as a retractor. The bowel loops are packed away for open surgical work.

The surgeon observes the inflamed colon and identifies a healthy segment of rectum where the tinea have splayed. A contour stapler with a green load is used to transect the rectum, ensuring no other tissues are caught in the jaws. The surgeon confirms the ureter and vagina are clear before firing the stapler. The diseased colon is removed, revealing a soft and healthy rectal segment. Fluorescence angiography with ICG is used to assess the blood supply, showing good vascularity for potential anastomosis.

The surgeon creates a window in the mesentery and uses a ligature to take the remaining meso. A diverticulum is identified and excised to prevent weakness in the anastomosis. The surgeon prepares for an open anastomosis by ensuring the mesentery is straight and clear of fat, using a purse-string technique to secure the colotomy opening. The EEA stapler is positioned with the spike brought out, ensuring proper alignment for the anastomosis.

The surgeon inserts a 29 EEA stapler through the rectum, ensuring it is not mistakenly placed into the vagina. The stapler is positioned on the staple line, and the mesentery is straightened before firing. After firing, the surgeon checks for complete doughnuts, confirming two thick, complete doughnuts. The pelvis is irrigated, and a FlexSig is used for a leak test, showing no air bubbles and a well-perfused, hemostatic anastomosis at ten centimeters from the anus. The procedure concludes with the removal of trocars and preparation for closure.
